# Obsidian API Feasibility

The core question: **can a true-outliner experience be built with public plugin APIs only —
no private APIs, no monkey-patching, a clean automated-review scorecard?**

Short answer: **yes for the editor-centric architecture; no (today) for a Kanban-style
custom-view takeover of `.md` files.** Details below.

## The constraint set ("perfect scorecard")

As of 2026 Obsidian runs a [Community directory with automated review](https://obsidian.md/blog/future-of-plugins/):
every plugin *version* is scanned for security and code quality, and each plugin page shows a
**safety scorecard** (with planned disclosure of network/filesystem/clipboard access). Manual
review continues for popular plugins. Compliance is checkable locally via the official
[`obsidianmd/eslint-plugin`](https://github.com/obsidianmd/eslint-plugin).

**What the scorecard empirically measures** (decomposed from a real red one — see the
workflowy-style-outline case study in the plugin-landscape doc). Two independent badges:
*Health* (activity: readme/license hygiene, commit/release cadence, issue responsiveness,
adoption) and *Review* (automated scans of each release). The Review scans are, concretely:
(1) capability detection surfaced as user-facing disclosures — vault read/write, network
calls, clipboard, base64 usage; (2) dependency vulnerability scan; (3) malware/obfuscation/
network-behavior scans gated on **build verification, which requires a committed lockfile**;
(4) the obsidianmd + strict-TypeScript eslint rules; (5) CSS lints (`!important`, `:has`);
(6) release hygiene (GitHub artifact attestations, release notes, manifest/release
consistency). Monkey-patching/private-API use is *not* among the automated checks — it
remains a manual-review and fragility concern, not a scorecard line item.

**Green-scorecard checklist for this project (day 1):** eslint-plugin-obsidianmd + strict
typescript-eslint in CI; zero `!important`/`:has` (style via Obsidian CSS variables and
specificity); committed lockfile + GitHub artifact attestations + real release notes in the
release workflow; no network/clipboard capability at all (vault-only footprint → near-empty
disclosure panel); no deprecated APIs (`activeLeaf`, `execCommand`, …).

Relevant hard rules from the [Developer policies](https://docs.obsidian.md/Developer+policies)
and [Plugin guidelines](https://docs.obsidian.md/Plugins/Releasing/Plugin+guidelines):

- No telemetry, no ads, no self-updating, no code obfuscation.
- Security: no `innerHTML`/`outerHTML` with user data — build DOM via `createEl()` etc.
- Vault API over Adapter; `Editor` API for active-note edits; `Vault.process()` for background
  edits; `processFrontMatter()` for YAML; `normalizePath()`; no `workspace.activeLeaf` access;
  register everything for auto-cleanup; no default hotkeys; sentence case UI; no Node/Electron
  APIs if mobile is supported.

Note: the policies do not *explicitly* ban private-API use — but monkey-patching internals is
exactly what the code-quality scan and reviewers look for, it's the community's #1 breakage
source, and the project's own bar ("no private APIs or hacks") rules it out regardless.

## What Obsidian already gives us (public, documented)

This list is the pleasant surprise of the research — the platform provides more outliner
primitives than any of the reference apps had to start from:

1. **The entire CodeMirror 6 extension surface is official API.**
   `Plugin.registerEditorExtension()` accepts arbitrary CM6 extensions: `StateField`,
   `ViewPlugin`, `keymap` (with `Prec.highest`), **`transactionFilter` / `transactionExtender`**
   (veto or rewrite any document/selection change → this is the enforcement mechanism),
   `Decoration.replace/widget/line` (hide syntax, draw node chrome, block widgets),
   `atomicRanges` (make bullet markers cursor-atomic), fold service, drop handlers.
   obsidian-outliner, obsidian-zoom and pro-outliner jointly prove Enter/Tab grammar, stick-to-
   content cursors, subtree hiding (zoom), selection limiting, selection expansion, drag-and-
   drop, mirrors, and breadcrumb headers all work through this surface.
2. **Core parses list structure for the whole vault already.** `CachedMetadata.listItems` gives
   every list item's span and **`parent` pointer** (plus task state) for every markdown file,
   kept fresh by the metadata cache — a vault-wide outline index for free. Combined with
   `resolvedLinks` (also public), this is everything needed for structured backlinks *without
   touching private search internals*.
3. **Block identity is native.** `^block-id` IDs, `[[note#^id]]` links, `![[note#^id]]` embeds
   are core Obsidian markdown — indexed (`CachedMetadata.blocks`), rendered, hover-previewed
   and search-integrated by core. A true outliner can adopt them as its node-reference syntax
   and inherit the whole ecosystem's compatibility, instead of inventing `id::` pollution
   (Logseq's mistake) or `((refs))`.
4. **Folding, Live Preview, `foldInfo`** — native list folding in the editor;
   `MarkdownView`/`editor:toggle-fold`; Live Preview already demonstrates syntax-hiding UX.
5. **Workspace/view APIs** — `registerView` for our own panes (structured backlinks, breadcrumb
   bar via `addStatusBarItem`/view header, etc.), `ItemView`, `MarkdownRenderer.render` for
   rendering node content in our panes.
6. **Events + `Vault.process`** for cross-file operations (mirror sync, refactors) done safely.

## Architecture options

### Option A — Editor-centric: CM6 extensions inside the standard markdown view ("recommended")

The markdown view stays; the plugin makes it *behave* like an outliner for outline documents:

- **Enforcement**: a `transactionFilter` normalizes every transaction — deletions crossing node
  boundaries become structural deletions; pastes are re-indented to valid depth; a line can
  never lose its bullet while in outline mode; selection changes escalate to node selection when
  crossing boundaries (pro-outliner already ships a v1 of this).
- **Grammar**: high-precedence keymaps for Enter/Shift+Enter/Tab/moves (proven).
- **Node UX**: decorations for bullets/handles/fold chevrons; block-select highlight via line
  decorations; zoom via replace-decorations + breadcrumbs (proven by obsidian-zoom).
- **Scope control**: extensions are registered globally but no-op unless the file is an outline
  document (frontmatter flag / folder / global mode — open question), checked via metadataCache.

*Pros*: 100% public API; perfect-scorecard compatible; mobile works; degrades gracefully (files
are plain markdown lists everywhere else); interops with the whole ecosystem (backlinks, graph,
search, sync, other plugins). *Cons*: we inherit markdown-view constraints — the DOM is CM6's
line-based DOM, so some pixel-perfect block UI (e.g., Notion-style hover menus per block) takes
more creativity; "hard" enforcement is a filter over a text buffer, which demands a rigorous,
well-tested invariant layer (this is exactly the pure tree-ops + property-test discipline from
the TUI prototype).

### Option B — Custom view: parse md → own block editor (Kanban/Excalidraw pattern)

A `TextFileView` renders the outline as a fully custom (e.g., per-node contenteditable/CM)
block UI; the file stays markdown on disk.

The blocker: **there is no public mechanism to open `.md` files in a custom view.**
Kanban does it by monkey-patching `WorkspaceLeaf.setViewState` (confirmed in source: intercepts
`state.type === 'markdown'`, checks for its frontmatter key, rewrites to its view type) — a
private-API hack, fragile, and contrary to this project's bar. A
[2025 forum thread](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/custom-viewer-ui-for-certain-markdown-files/108104)
asking for an official alternative got no answer from the team. Alternatives all lose something
essential: a custom extension (`.outline`) forfeits metadataCache/links/graph/mobile-editor
interop; "open via command" per file is UX friction and re-breaks on every workspace restore.

*Nuance discovered later (2026-07-13, from the
[workflowy-style-outline](https://github.com/springrain1/workflowy-style-outline) case study —
see the plugin-landscape doc)*: the monkey-patch is only needed for **automatic** association.
An **explicit, user-invoked toggle** — a command/menu item calling
`leaf.setViewState({type: OUR_VIEW})` on the active leaf — is plain public API, and workspace
persistence keeps a toggled leaf in that view across restarts. So a scorecard-clean custom view
is *reachable*; what remains against Option B is everything else that project demonstrates:
rebuilding the editor (per-block textareas, IME/mobile/undo/save-queue re-implementations,
an "isolation layer" to avoid breaking native Obsidian) and, in their case, a lossy
md↔outline conversion — the anti-example for our isomorphism requirement.

*Verdict*: keep as a possible far-future layer (the explicit-toggle variant lowers the API
barrier but not the engineering cost); not a foundation compatible with today's goals.

### Option C — Hybrid (A + owned side surfaces)

Option A for editing, plus our own `registerView` panes where we fully control rendering:
**structured backlinks pane** (tree context from `listItems`, editable via `Vault.process` or
embedded editors), zoom breadcrumb header, optional outline-wide search/filter view. All public.
This captures most of what Option B wanted anyway, without the takeover problem.

## Feasibility scorecard per target feature

| Target behavior | Public-API path | Confidence |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Enter/Tab/move/merge grammar | CM6 keymaps (proven in prod) | High |
| Structure enforcement | `transactionFilter` invariant layer | High (novel engineering, proven primitives) |
| Node selection model | transactionFilter + decorations (pro-outliner v1 exists) | High |
| Fold + persistence | native folds; storage decision open (plugin data vs in-file) | High |
| Zoom + breadcrumbs | replace-decorations (proven: obsidian-zoom) | High |
| Drag & drop | CM6 drop + widgets (proven: obsidian-outliner) | High |
| Multiline nodes | md continuation lines + grammar keymaps | Medium-high (careful spec needed) |
| Block refs/embeds | native `^id` / `![[#^id]]` | High (core does the heavy lifting) |
| Mirrors (synced blocks) | native embeds first; true editable mirrors later | Medium |
| Structured backlinks pane (read) | own view + `resolvedLinks` + `listItems` | High |
| Editable backlinks | + `Vault.process`/embedded editor per block | Medium |
| Mobile | all of the above is CM6/DOM | High (test discipline) |
| Perfect scorecard | eslint-plugin from day 1; no `any`-casts into internals | High under Option A |

Two honesty notes: (a) `getBacklinksForFile` is **not** public — the public path is
`resolvedLinks` + own reverse index (the [backlink-cache](https://github.com/mnaoumov/obsidian-backlink-cache)
plugin shows the pattern); (b) even obsidian-outliner reaches for `(editor as any).cm` — we can
avoid it almost everywhere by living inside registered extensions (a `ViewPlugin` receives its
`EditorView` directly), which should be an explicit engineering rule.

## Addendum: the universal isomorphic outline view (post-alignment)

The 2026-07-12 alignment (see [04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md), Q2) widened the
document model from "lists" to the **full markdown block tree** — headings, paragraphs, code
fences, quotes, tables *and* list items are all nodes; the outline is a toggleable editor UI
over any note, with a lossless (isomorphic) round-trip. Feasibility notes for that vision:

- **The parse already exists.** `CachedMetadata.sections` (typed top-level blocks with
  positions), `headings` (text + level + position) and `listItems` (span + parent pointer)
  together describe exactly this block tree for every file in the vault, maintained by core.
  The editor side can parse incrementally from the CM6 document (and/or the bundled
  `@codemirror/language` syntax tree) for keystroke-latency accuracy; the cache serves
  cross-file features.
- **The CM6 architecture is unchanged.** Enforcement (`transactionFilter`), grammar (keymaps),
  node chrome (decorations), zoom (replace-decorations) apply to heading/paragraph nodes the
  same way they apply to list items. Obsidian's own fold system already treats *both* headings
  and list indents as foldable trees — precedent inside the core editor itself.
- **The new hard problem is the mapping algebra, not the APIs.** Headings encode depth by
  level, lists by indentation, paragraphs by adjacency — three different depth encodings in one
  tree. Operations must be closed over "trees expressible as natural markdown" (e.g.
  paragraph-under-paragraph has no markdown form → the op must convert, reject, or remap;
  h6 bounds heading depth). This is a spec/design problem — precisely what the OpenSpec
  explore/design phase is for — not an API gap.
- **Prior art**: org-mode is the closest living system (heading tree + plain lists with
  subtree promote/demote/move); no Obsidian plugin attempts the unified mapping. Obsidian's
  own "fold anything" + `sections` cache suggest the platform was shaped by adjacent thinking.

## Bottom line

The desired experience is achievable **within the rules** via Option A/C. The differentiating
work is not exotic API access — it's (1) a rigorously specified and tested **invariant/
enforcement layer** over CM6 transactions, (2) a coherent **node-selection model**, and (3) a
**structured backlinks pane** built on the vault-wide list index. The reference precedents
(outliner, zoom, pro-outliner, influx) collectively de-risk nearly every primitive; no existing
plugin has assembled them into one enforced, coherent product.

content.text:
I have a full picture of the local history now. The key finding so far: the code in `decorate.ts`/`decorations.ts`/`styles.css` actually implements "attempt 3" (decorate *every* kind uniformly), but `design.md`/`spec.md`/`tasks.md` in the working tree describe the earlier "paragraph-only" walk-back — the docs and code have diverged, and `decorations.ts` even cites a "D9/D10" that doesn't exist in the current `design.md`. That's a process-integrity problem layered on top of the technical one.

Before drawing conclusions, I want real external grounding rather than relying on the postmortem's secondhand summary. I'll dispatch three parallel research agents: one to verify obsidian-outliner's actual technique in source, one on Logseq's rendering architecture (block-based vs. shared-buffer — I suspect this is the crux), and one on broader CM6/editor prior art for cross-kind indentation.

input.prompt:
I'm researching prior art for an Obsidian plugin that needs to draw tree-depth indent guides and node markers across a CodeMirror 6 (CM6) editor buffer. A prior implementation attempt (CSS `padding-left`/`text-indent`/`margin-left` overrides with `!important` on `.cm-line`, driven by a `--to-depth` custom property) broke badly on real content: it fought Obsidian's native list hanging-indent (`text-indent`+`padding-left` pair), needed `!important` to beat cascade, had `em`-vs-`rem` unit bugs (marker size scaling with heading font-size), and collided with Obsidian's fold-indicator chevron when trying to reposition list bullets. I need to deeply verify and expand on what obsidian-outliner (github.com/vslinko/obsidian-outliner) actually does, since a prior research pass (via DeepWiki, high-level only) claimed its vertical indent lines are NOT CSS but a `ViewPlugin` that calls `view.coordsAtPos()`/`lineBlockAt()` to read already-rendered pixel positions and draws absolutely-positioned overlay `<div>`s on top — i.e. it measures post-layout pixels rather than fighting the box model.

Please dig into the actual source (via DeepWiki ask_question/read_wiki_contents on vslinko/obsidian-outliner, and/or by fetching the actual GitHub source files directly — e.g. look for files with "indent", "line", "guide", "style" in the name under src/) and answer with concrete file/line references where possible:

1. Confirm (or correct) the coordsAtPos/lineBlockAt overlay-div claim for vertical indent guide lines. What CSS properties does the overlay actually use (position, left, top, height, width, border/background)? Is it recomputed on every ViewUpdate or something more targeted (viewport-only, docChanged-only)?
2. Does obsidian-outliner (or its forks obsidian-zoom / obsidian-pro-outliner / kdnk/obsidian-bullet) EVER apply any indentation or marker styling to non-list content — headings, plain paragraphs, code fences? Or is every single mechanism strictly scoped to `<li>`/list-item lines only? I need a definitive answer since my working hypothesis is that no existing Obsidian plugin has attempted "unify heading/paragraph/list into one visual indentation language" — only list-internal styling.
3. How does it restyle/replace the native bullet glyph (if at all)? Any `font-size`/`em` tricks, and if so, do they use `rem` or otherwise avoid the "marker size scales with the font-size of whatever line it's on" bug class? Or does it avoid touching bullet size/shape entirely?
4. Does it use `!important` anywhere in its stylesheet? How does it achieve specificity over Obsidian's own `.cm-line` rules if not?
5. How does it handle indent-guide positioning for wrapped/soft-line-broken or multi-line list items (continuation lines)? Does the guide line span the full rendered height of a multi-line item, and how is that height obtained?
6. Does it disable Obsidian's own native `.cm-indent::before` dashes when its own guides are active (a mode class), and how exactly?
7. Any use of `Decoration.widget` with a spacer/inline element (as opposed to CSS padding/margin/text-indent overrides) anywhere in its indentation mechanism — i.e. does it ever "add real rendered width via a widget" rather than override a CSS box-model property?

Report back with concrete, source-grounded findings (cite file paths/line numbers or exact quoted code where you can), organized by the 7 numbered questions above. Flag clearly anything you could not verify from source and had to infer. Keep total length under ~700 words of prose plus any short code quotes.

I'm evaluating whether Logseq's visual "uniform outline" look (every block — regardless of whether its content looks like a heading, a paragraph, or a list item — gets the same fat round bullet and consistent per-level indentation) is a technique that could transfer to a very different kind of editor: a single shared CodeMirror 6 (CM6) text buffer showing raw markdown (Obsidian's Live Preview editor), where headings/paragraphs/list-items are three structurally different markdown encodings sharing one continuous document, each with their own native CSS rendering (headings: distinct typography, no indentation; paragraphs: no indentation, no marker; list items: native hanging-indent via a `text-indent`+`padding-left` CSS pair plus a native bullet/number glyph) — as opposed to Logseq's actual architecture.

My working hypothesis, which I need you to confirm, deny, or refine with real evidence: Logseq's uniform look is achievable *because* every block in Logseq's data model is fundamentally the same kind of thing — a "block" with content and children — and there is no separate "heading" or "paragraph" render-kind at all; something that looks like a heading in Logseq is just a block whose text happens to start with `#` markdown syntax styled larger, but it still gets the exact same bullet, same indent mechanics, same per-block DOM treatment as every other block. If true, this means Logseq never had to solve "make a heading and a list item and a plain paragraph share one indentation language despite being different native constructs" — it sidestepped the problem by giving every block identical structural treatment from the start, at the data-model level, not just the rendering level.

Please research (web search + fetching Logseq's own architecture docs/blog posts + if useful, github.com/logseq/logseq source or its docs for how blocks/pages are rendered) and answer:

1. Is my hypothesis correct? Does Logseq's data model / editor really treat every block identically regardless of whether its content "looks like" a heading vs. a paragraph vs. a list item — i.e. is there truly no separate heading-node or paragraph-node concept distinct from a generic block?
2. Concretely, how does Logseq render a page: is it one big contentEditable/textarea for the whole page (shared buffer, like CM6), or is each block its own independent DOM element/component that's edited in isolation (e.g. a block is static rendered HTML until focused, then swapped for an editable textarea just for that one block, un-swapped on blur)? I need to know whether Logseq's uniformity is fundamentally a consequence of a "one component per block" architecture rather than "one shared buffer with clever CSS."
3. When a Logseq "file-based" (OG) markdown export is inspected, how does a Logseq heading actually get serialized to markdown? Does it use standard `#`/`##` ATX heading syntax, or does everything (including things that look like headings in the UI) get written out as a `-` bulleted list item with heading-level markdown syntax INSIDE the bullet's text? This matters a lot for the "isomorphism with existing plain markdown" question.
4. Any official Logseq engineering blog posts, changelog entries, or design docs discussing WHY they chose per-block components over a shared text buffer, or any tradeoffs they mention (performance, editing complexity, IME issues, etc.)?
5. Briefly (a paragraph is enough): do you know of similar info for Roam Research or Tana's rendering architecture — do they also use one-component-per-block, reinforcing that this is the standard approach among "true outliner" apps, as opposed to a shared-text-buffer editor like CM6/Obsidian?

Report back organized by the 5 numbered questions, with sources cited (URLs). Flag anything you couldn't verify and had to infer or where sources conflict. Keep it under ~700 words of prose.

input.description: CM6 cross-kind indentation prior art research
input.prompt:
I'm researching implementation techniques for a hard sub-problem in a CodeMirror 6 (CM6)-based markdown editor (this is Obsidian's Live Preview editor, extended by a plugin via the public `registerEditorExtension`/`Decoration`/`ViewPlugin` API): making three structurally different markdown encodings — ATX headings (`#`/`##`), plain paragraphs (no native indentation or marker at all), and list items (native CSS hanging-indent via a `text-indent`(negative)+`padding-left` pair, plus a native bullet/number glyph widget) — all visually read as nodes in ONE consistent tree-depth indentation hierarchy, without breaking Obsidian's own native rendering of any of them. A prior attempt tried overriding `padding-left`/`text-indent`/`margin-left` directly via CSS `!important` on every line uniformly by computed tree depth, and it broke: it fought Obsidian's own hanging-indent math, needed increasingly specific selectors, had `em`-vs-`rem` unit-resolution bugs, and collided with Obsidian's fold-indicator chevron when trying to reposition list bullets.

I want to know if there's a fundamentally different, more robust technique than "override the CSS box-model property and hope specificity/units are right everywhere." Please research the following and report back with concrete, sourced findings:

1. **CM6 `Decoration.widget` as a "spacer" technique**: is there known prior art (blog posts, CM6 official examples/docs, GitHub repos) for inserting an inline (not block) widget decoration at the very start of a line's rendered content — an invisible/empty `<span>` with a computed pixel width — to push the rest of the line's content rightward via real layout width, as an alternative to `padding-left`/`text-indent`? Does this actually work as I'd expect (participates in normal inline flow, doesn't fight existing CSS, isn't included in copy/paste text since widgets aren't part of the document's text content)? Any known gotchas?
2. **Emacs `org-indent-mode`**: this is prior art from a completely different editor (Emacs) for a very similar problem — visually indenting a heading's body text to match its outline depth without touching the actual buffer content. It's documented to use `line-prefix`/`wrap-prefix` text properties (which insert virtual, non-buffer characters before each visual line for indentation purposes) rather than any box-model CSS-equivalent. Research exactly how this works (line-prefix vs wrap-prefix, what it renders, performance notes, any known bugs/limitations reported over the years) as a conceptual reference. Is there a rough CM6 equivalent to `line-prefix`, or is the widget-decoration approach in question 1 the closest available analog?
3. **CSS indent-guide techniques from code editors**: research whether there's a well-known CSS technique (used in VSCode, other code editors/CM6 indent-guide plugins/npm packages like a CM6 "indentation markers" extension) for drawing MULTIPLE vertical indent-guide lines per code line (one per ancestor nesting level) via a single element using stacked `background-image: repeating-linear-gradient(...)` or multiple `box-shadow` layers, rather than one DOM pseudo-element per guide line. Find and describe the technique concretely (I want to know if this is more robust/composable than a single `::before` per line).
4. **Silverbullet** (github.com/silverbulletmd/silverbullet) — a CM6-based personal-knowledge-management/note app, conceptually similar to Obsidian but web-based and open source. Does it have any decoration/rendering feature that unifies heading/paragraph/list visual treatment, or per-block "fat bullet"-style chrome? Check its source/docs for anything relevant to cross-kind block decoration in a shared CM6 buffer.
5. Any other CM6-based note-taking/outliner tools (Foam, Dendron, Athens Research (if CM6-based), any "org-mode for CM6" projects) that attempt a similar unification, and how they did it.

Report organized by the 5 numbered questions, concrete and source-grounded (cite URLs), flag anything inferred vs. confirmed. Keep it under ~700 words of prose plus short code/CSS quotes where illustrative.

Now I have everything needed for a source-grounded answer.

## Findings: vslinko/obsidian-outliner indent-guide mechanism

**Source verified directly** (raw GitHub fetch of `master` branch, not just DeepWiki): `src/features/VerticalLines.ts` (314 lines), `src/features/BetterListsStyles.ts`, `src/features/DragAndDrop.ts`, and the complete `styles.css` (105 lines, root of repo).

**1. coordsAtPos/lineBlockAt overlay claim — CONFIRMED, with mechanics.**
`VerticalLinesPluginValue` (a `ViewPlugin`, `VerticalLines.ts:27`) computes each guide line's box using `this.view.coordsAtPos(fromOffset, 1)` (line 171, for `left`) and `this.view.lineBlockAt(fromOffset).top` / `.bottom` (lines 180, 183-184, for vertical extent). Results are pushed into a `lines: LineData[]` array, then `updateDom()` (line 261) writes `el.style.top/left/height` directly as **inline styles** on absolutely-positioned `<div class="outliner-plugin-list-line">` elements appended into a `contentContainer` → `scroller` overlay that's a sibling appended to `view.dom` (not inside `.cm-line`). Recompute is **not** on every `ViewUpdate` unconditionally: `update(update: ViewUpdate)` (line 82) only calls `scheduleRecalculate()` when `docChanged || viewportChanged || geometryChanged || transactions.some(reconfigured)`, and that schedules via `setTimeout(calculate, 0)` (debounced to next tick). Plain scrolling does **not** trigger recompute — a separate `scroll` listener (line 42) just does `this.scroller.scrollTo(scrollLeft, scrollTop)` to mirror the editor's scroll offset on the overlay, cheap and decoupled from recalculation.

**2. Scoped strictly to list content — CONFIRMED, no heading/paragraph/code-fence styling found.**
Entire `styles.css` selectors touching non-overlay content are `.HyperMD-list-line`, `.cm-formatting-list-ul`, `.list-bullet::after`, `.cm-hmd-list-indent .cm-indent::before` — all CodeMirror/Obsidian classes that exist only on actual list-item lines. `VerticalLines.ts`'s `recursive()` walks a parsed `List`/`getChildren()` tree and bails (`children.length === 0`) on non-list nodes. Nothing in the codebase applies indentation/marker CSS to headings or paragraphs. (Confirmed independently via DeepWiki `ask_question` against the live repo, which found the same.)

**3. Bullet restyling — uses `em`, not `rem`, but the em-scaling bug class doesn't apply here.**
`.outliner-plugin-better-lists .list-bullet::after { width: 0.4em; height: 0.4em; background-color: var(--text-muted); }`. <result>I have enough to report. Silverbullet does not implement a Logseq/Workflowy-style unified "fat bullet" chrome across block kinds.

**What Silverbullet actually has:**
- `client/codemirror/list.ts` — a `listBulletPlugin()` (registered via `cleanModePlugins` in `client/codemirror/clean.ts`) that uses `Decoration.replace` + a `ListBulletWidget` to swap the raw `-`/`*`/`+` marker for a `•` glyph (class `cm-list-bullet`), reverting to source text when the cursor is on that line. This is list-item-only.
- `client/codemirror/editor_state.ts` — a `lineWrapper` extension that tags each line with a CSS class from the syntax tree (`sb-line-h1`…`sb-line-h6`, `sb-line-li`, `sb-line-blockquote`, etc.), styled in `client/styles/editor.scss`/`colors.scss`. This gives headings/blockquotes/etc. distinct *typographic* styling (size, color, indent via `text-indent`/`padding-left` for things like comments), but no shared glyph/marker chrome — no bullet-like widget is attached to headings or plain paragraphs.
- SilverBullet's own docs are explicit about this: the [Outlines](https://silverbullet.md/Outlines) page states **"While SilverBullet is not a 'proper' outliner like e.g. LogSeq, it does offer useful commands to manage outlines."** Its "Outlines" feature is a set of keyboard commands (`Mod-. h/l/j/k` etc.) for moving/indenting/folding headings, list items, table rows, and paragraphs — a unified *command* interface, not unified *visual* presentation.
- Community/GitHub evidence backs this reading: a [community thread "Coming from Logseq"](https://community.silverbullet.md/t/coming-from-logseq-outlines-and-linked-mentions/290) discusses only functional/workflow gaps (link inheritance, live query editing), never visual bullet unification. [Issue #1936](https://github.com/silverbulletmd/silverbullet/issues/1936) is about treating `-`/`*` bullets as structurally equivalent for move operations, not about rendering.

**Bottom line:** Silverbullet does NOT unify heading/paragraph/list visual treatment the way Logseq/Workflowy do. Only list items get a decorated bullet glyph; headings and paragraphs get CSS class-based typographic styling with no shared marker/indent-as-node chrome. No blog posts, HN threads, or forum discussions were found specifically debating this design question for Silverbullet.

Sources: https://github.com/silverbulletmd/silverbullet/blob/main/client/codemirror/list.ts, https://github.com/silverbulletmd/silverbullet/blob/main/client/codemirror/editor_state.ts, https://github.com/silverbulletmd/silverbullet/blob/main/client/codemirror/clean.ts, https://silverbullet.md/Outlines, https://community.silverbullet.md/t/coming-from-logseq-outlines-and-linked-mentions/290, https://github.com/silverbulletmd/silverbullet/issues/1936</result>

<result># Research Report: Single-Element Multi-Layer CSS Indent Guides

**1. VS Code — NOT this technique (confirmed).** VS Code renders indent guides via DOM, one `<div>` per guide level per line, not stacked layers on one element. In `IndentGuidesOverlay.prepareRender()`:
```
result += `<div class="core-guide ${guide.className} ${className}" style="left:${left}px;width:${width}px"></div>`;
```
Each div gets a single-layer visual line via `box-shadow: 1px 0 0 0 var(--indent-color) inset;` (and `border-top`/`border-bottom` for bracket-guide horizontal segments), registered in `registerThemingParticipant`. So N ancestor levels = N `<div>`s per line, each with one (not stacked) box-shadow. No canvas is used for indent guides specifically.
Source: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/src/vs/editor/browser/viewParts/indentGuides/indentGuides.ts

**2. CM6 `@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers` — CONFIRMED single-element/stacked-gradient technique.** One line-decoration (`class: 'cm-indent-markers'`) per line sets a CSS custom property to a comma-joined list of `repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers (one per marker segment: before-active, active, after-active), consumed by a single `::before`:
```js
const gradient = `repeating-linear-gradient(to right, var(${markerCssProperty}) 0 ${thickness}px, transparent ${thickness}px ${indentWidth}ch)`
```
```css
'.cm-indent-markers::before': { position: 'absolute', background: 'var(--indent-markers)', ... }
```
So it's O(1) DOM elements per line regardless of depth. Source: https://github.com/replit/codemirror-indentation-markers/blob/master/src/index.ts

**3. Other concrete examples — not found (flag).** Search turned up only generic multi-box-shadow docs (https://github.com/jbranchaud/til/blob/master/css/apply-multiple-box-shadows-to-single-element.md, https://css-tricks.com/snippets/css/css-box-shadow/), not one applied specifically to indent guides.

**4. Assessment (my reasoning, inferred):** stacked-layer wins on DOM-node count (O(1) vs O(depth)) and ease of depth changes (regenerate one string vs create/destroy nodes); it loses on CSS-declarative flexibility — colors/widths are baked into a JS-computed string rather than overridable per-level via CSS classes (VS Code's per-div approach supports 6 rotating bracket colors purely via `lvl-N` selectors). Both scale fine with variable line height since extents use relative/`ch` units anchored to the line box.</result>

content.text:
# Logseq's Uniform-Outline Look: Data Model vs. Rendering Architecture

## 1. Is the hypothesis correct?

**Confirmed, with one nuance.** Logseq's persisted schema has no distinct heading-node or paragraph-node type. "Heading-ness" is a property (`:logseq.property/heading`, an int 1–6 or boolean) attached to an otherwise generic block entity. The `#`/`##` prefix is parsed out of the block's content string (`markdown-heading-level`, `wrap-parse-block` in `frontend/handler/db_based/editor.cljs`) and stored as that property; a block without it is just rendered as a paragraph — there's no separate paragraph type either. This is corroborated independently by Karl Voit's outside-user analysis: "everything — by default — is something that is a heading in [Org-mode] syntax. Classic itemize lists, normal paragraphs, tables … are a bit of a pain if possible at all" ([karl-voit.at](https://karl-voit.at/2024/01/28/logseq-from-org-pov/)).

Nuance: the underlying `mldoc` parser (shared with Org-mode support) *does* produce distinct `Heading`/`Paragraph` AST node kinds transiently during parsing (`extract-blocks`/`construct-block` in `graph-parser/block.cljs`). Logseq's ingestion step immediately collapses both into the same generic block record, keeping only the heading level as a property. So the type distinction exists for a moment inside the parser, but is erased at the data-model/persistence layer — exactly where your hypothesis says it matters. (Source: DeepWiki analysis of `logseq/logseq`, cross-checked against file contents I could not directly grep myself — flagging as AI-summarized, not hand-verified against raw source.)

## 2. One shared buffer, or one component per block?

**One component per block, confirmed.** Each block is wrapped in a `block-container`; a `block-content-or-editor` component toggles between static `block-content` (rendered HTML, via `markup-elements-cp`) and an editable `editor-box`/`ls-textarea`, driven by a `frontend.state` atom (`:editor/editing?` holding the focused block's UUID, set via `state/set-editing!`, cleared via `editor-on-hide` on blur/Escape). This is architecturally the opposite of CM6: not a shared buffer with clever CSS, but N independent DOM subtrees, only one of which is ever "live text" at a time.

Important wrinkle for your transfer question: even with full per-block DOM control, Logseq *still* needs bespoke CSS to keep the bullet vertically aligned against oversized heading text — `.block-title-wrap.as-heading` and a `top` offset on `.block-control-wrap` for h1/h2, verified by e2e tests (`multiline-heading-centers-bullet-on-first-heading-line`). So "uniform bullet+indent chrome" was not free even for Logseq; it required targeted CSS on top of the uniform component structure. That's a data point suggesting some CSS accommodation is likely unavoidable in any implementation, architecture aside.

## 3. Markdown serialization: bullet-wrapped, not ATX

**Confirmed.** A heading block serializes as `- ## Heading block` — bullet marker first, heading markdown nested inside — never as a standalone ATX line. This is explicit in ADR 0016 ("Blocks are Markdown list items that use `-`") and its test suite, and matches external descriptions of the older file-based format: "the outline structure translates to an unordered list … Headings are just list items" ([randomgeekery.org](https://randomgeekery.org/post/2022/03/logseqs-export-formats/)). Community export tools ([logseq-export](https://github.com/viktomas/logseq-export), [logseq-to-markdown](https://github.com/dom8509/logseq-to-markdown)) offer a "strip bullets" option specifically because bullets are the default. This behavior appears consistent across both the original file-based (OG) architecture and the new 2026 DB-based "Markdown Mirror" export ([ADR 0016](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/logseq/logseq/master/docs/adr/0016-markdown-mirror.md), dated 2026-05-05).

## 4. Official rationale for per-block components?

**Not found — flagging as unverified.** Logseq's public ADRs (`docs/adr/0001`–`0020`) only start in 2026 and cover DB-sync, undo/redo, and a Rum→hooks migration, not the original editor architecture decision (which predates ADR practice; Logseq's editor was built in ClojureScript/Rum around 2020). ADR 0018 (rum-hooks-refactor) calls "editor focus and cursor handling, block tree rendering" among the "most sensitive cases," implicitly acknowledging complexity, but states no original rationale. I found no blog post from Tienson Qin or blog.logseq.com discussing shared-buffer vs. per-block tradeoffs explicitly.

## 5. Roam and Tana

For Roam, community sources (not an official engineering blog — I found none) consistently describe the same swap pattern: a block shows rendered content until clicked, then reveals raw markdown in an editable field, reverting on blur ([Roam-Research/issues#575](https://github.com/Roam-Research/issues/issues/575)); Roam is closed-source, so this is corroborated only by user reports, not source inspection. Tana's public docs describe "everything is a node" ([tana.inc/articles/intro-to-nodes-fields-and-supertags](https://tana.inc/articles/intro-to-nodes-fields-and-supertags)) — consistent with a uniform per-node data model — but Tana is closed-source and I found no documentation confirming its DOM/rendering implementation specifically, so this is inferred by analogy, not verified.

I'm evaluating whether Logseq's visual "uniform outline" look (every block — regardless of whether its content looks like a heading, a paragraph, or a list item — gets the same fat round bullet and consistent per-level indentation) is a technique that could transfer to a very different kind of editor: a single shared CodeMirror 6 (CM6) text buffer showing raw markdown (Obsidian's Live Preview editor), where headings/paragraphs/list-items are three structurally different markdown encodings sharing one continuous document, each with their own native CSS rendering (headings: distinct typography, no indentation; paragraphs: no indentation, no marker; list items: native hanging-indent via a `text-indent`+`padding-left` CSS pair plus a native bullet/number glyph) — as opposed to Logseq's actual architecture.

My working hypothesis, which I need you to confirm, deny, or refine with real evidence: Logseq's uniform look is achievable *because* every block in Logseq's data model is fundamentally the same kind of thing — a "block" with content and children — and there is no separate "heading" or "paragraph" render-kind at all; something that looks like a heading in Logseq is just a block whose text happens to start with `#` markdown syntax styled larger, but it still gets the exact same bullet, same indent mechanics, same per-block DOM treatment as every other block. If true, this means Logseq never had to solve "make a heading and a list item and a plain paragraph share one indentation language despite being different native constructs" — it sidestepped the problem by giving every block identical structural treatment from the start, at the data-model level, not just the rendering level.

Please research (web search + fetching Logseq's own architecture docs/blog posts + if useful, github.com/logseq/logseq source or its docs for how blocks/pages are rendered) and answer:

1. Is my hypothesis correct? Does Logseq's data model / editor really treat every block identically regardless of whether its content "looks like" a heading vs. a paragraph vs. a list item — i.e. is there truly no separate heading-node or paragraph-node concept distinct from a generic block?
2. Concretely, how does Logseq render a page: is it one big contentEditable/textarea for the whole page (shared buffer, like CM6), or is each block its own independent DOM element/component that's edited in isolation (e.g. a block is static rendered HTML until focused, then swapped for an editable textarea just for that one block, un-swapped on blur)? I need to know whether Logseq's uniformity is fundamentally a consequence of a "one component per block" architecture rather than "one shared buffer with clever CSS."
3. When a Logseq "file-based" (OG) markdown export is inspected, how does a Logseq heading actually get serialized to markdown? Does it use standard `#`/`##` ATX heading syntax, or does everything (including things that look like headings in the UI) get written out as a `-` bulleted list item with heading-level markdown syntax INSIDE the bullet's text? This matters a lot for the "isomorphism with existing plain markdown" question.
4. Any official Logseq engineering blog posts, changelog entries, or design docs discussing WHY they chose per-block components over a shared text buffer, or any tradeoffs they mention (performance, editing complexity, IME issues, etc.)?
5. Briefly (a paragraph is enough): do you know of similar info for Roam Research or Tana's rendering architecture — do they also use one-component-per-block, reinforcing that this is the standard approach among "true outliner" apps, as opposed to a shared-text-buffer editor like CM6/Obsidian?

Report back organized by the 5 numbered questions, with sources cited (URLs). Flag anything you couldn't verify and had to infer or where sources conflict. Keep it under ~700 words of prose.

(Source: DeepWiki analysis of `logseq/logseq`, cross-checked against file contents I could not directly grep myself — flagging as AI-summarized, not hand-verified against raw source.)

## 2. One shared buffer, or one component per block?

**One component per block, confirmed.** Each block is wrapped in a `block-container`; a `block-content-or-editor` component toggles between static `block-content` (rendered HTML, via `markup-elements-cp`) and an editable `editor-box`/`ls-textarea`, driven by a `frontend.state` atom (`:editor/editing?` holding the focused block's UUID, set via `state/set-editing!`, cleared via `editor-on-hide` on blur/Escape). This is architecturally the opposite of CM6: not a shared buffer with clever CSS, but N independent DOM subtrees, only one of which is ever "live text" at a time.

Important wrinkle for your transfer question: even with full per-block DOM control, Logseq *still* needs bespoke CSS to keep the bullet vertically aligned against oversized heading text — `.block-title-wrap.as-heading` and a `top` offset on `.block-control-wrap` for h1/h2, verified by e2e tests (`multiline-heading-centers-bullet-on-first-heading-line`). So "uniform bullet+indent chrome" was not free even for Logseq; it required targeted CSS on top of the uniform component structure. That's a data point suggesting some CSS accommodation is likely unavoidable in any implementation, architecture aside.

## 3. Markdown serialization: bullet-wrapped, not ATX

**Confirmed.** A heading block serializes as `- ## Heading block` — bullet marker first, heading markdown nested inside — never as a standalone ATX line. This is explicit in ADR 0016 ("Blocks are Markdown list items that use `-`") and its test suite, and matches external descriptions of the older file-based format: "the outline structure translates to an unordered list … Headings are just list items" ([randomgeekery.org](https://randomgeekery.org/post/2022/03/logseqs-export-formats/)). Community export tools ([logseq-export](https://github.com/viktomas/logseq-export), [logseq-to-markdown](https://github.com/dom8509/logseq-to-markdown)) offer a "strip bullets" option specifically because bullets are the default. This behavior appears consistent across both the original file-based (OG) architecture and the new 2026 DB-based "Markdown Mirror" export ([ADR 0016](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/logseq/logseq/master/docs/adr/0016-markdown-mirror.md), dated 2026-05-05).

## 4. Official rationale for per-block components?

**Not found — flagging as unverified.** Logseq's public ADRs (`docs/adr/0001`–`0020`) only start in 2026 and cover DB-sync, undo/redo, and a Rum→hooks migration, not the original editor architecture decision (which predates ADR practice; Logseq's editor was built in ClojureScript/Rum around 2020). ADR 0018 (rum-hooks-refactor) calls "editor focus and cursor handling, block tree rendering" among the "most sensitive cases," implicitly acknowledging complexity, but states no original rationale. I found no blog post from Tienson Qin or blog.logseq.com discussing shared-buffer vs. per-block tradeoffs explicitly.

## 5. Roam and Tana

For Roam, community sources (not an official engineering blog — I found none) consistently describe the same swap pattern: a block shows rendered content until clicked, then reveals raw markdown in an editable field, reverting on blur ([Roam-Research/issues#575](https://github.com/Roam-Research/issues/issues/575)); Roam is closed-source, so this is corroborated only by user reports, not source inspection. Tana's public docs describe "everything is a node" ([tana.inc/articles/intro-to-nodes-fields-and-supertags](https://tana.inc/articles/intro-to-nodes-fields-and-supertags)) — consistent with a uniform per-node data model — but Tana is closed-source and I found no documentation confirming its DOM/rendering implementation specifically, so this is inferred by analogy, not verified.

<result>I have enough to report. Silverbullet does not implement a Logseq/Workflowy-style unified "fat bullet" chrome across block kinds.

**What Silverbullet actually has:**
- `client/codemirror/list.ts` — a `listBulletPlugin()` (registered via `cleanModePlugins` in `client/codemirror/clean.ts`) that uses `Decoration.replace` + a `ListBulletWidget` to swap the raw `-`/`*`/`+` marker for a `•` glyph (class `cm-list-bullet`), reverting to source text when the cursor is on that line. This is list-item-only.
- `client/codemirror/editor_state.ts` — a `lineWrapper` extension that tags each line with a CSS class from the syntax tree (`sb-line-h1`…`sb-line-h6`, `sb-line-li`, `sb-line-blockquote`, etc.), styled in `client/styles/editor.scss`/`colors.scss`. This gives headings/blockquotes/etc. distinct *typographic* styling (size, color, indent via `text-indent`/`padding-left` for things like comments), but no shared glyph/marker chrome — no bullet-like widget is attached to headings or plain paragraphs.
- SilverBullet's own docs are explicit about this: the [Outlines](https://silverbullet.md/Outlines) page states **"While SilverBullet is not a 'proper' outliner like e.g. LogSeq, it does offer useful commands to manage outlines."** Its "Outlines" feature is a set of keyboard commands (`Mod-. h/l/j/k` etc.) for moving/indenting/folding headings, list items, table rows, and paragraphs — a unified *command* interface, not unified *visual* presentation.
- Community/GitHub evidence backs this reading: a [community thread "Coming from Logseq"](https://community.silverbullet.md/t/coming-from-logseq-outlines-and-linked-mentions/290) discusses only functional/workflow gaps (link inheritance, live query editing), never visual bullet unification. [Issue #1936](https://github.com/silverbulletmd/silverbullet/issues/1936) is about treating `-`/`*` bullets as structurally equivalent for move operations, not about rendering.

**Bottom line:** Silverbullet does NOT unify heading/paragraph/list visual treatment the way Logseq/Workflowy do. Only list items get a decorated bullet glyph; headings and paragraphs get CSS class-based typographic styling with no shared marker/indent-as-node chrome. No blog posts, HN threads, or forum discussions were found specifically debating this design question for Silverbullet.

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result># Research Report: Single-Element Multi-Layer CSS Indent Guides

**1. VS Code — NOT this technique (confirmed).** VS Code renders indent guides via DOM, one `<div>` per guide level per line, not stacked layers on one element. In `IndentGuidesOverlay.prepareRender()`:
```
result += `<div class="core-guide ${guide.className} ${className}" style="left:${left}px;width:${width}px"></div>`;
```
Each div gets a single-layer visual line via `box-shadow: 1px 0 0 0 var(--indent-color) inset;` (and `border-top`/`border-bottom` for bracket-guide horizontal segments), registered in `registerThemingParticipant`. So N ancestor levels = N `<div>`s per line, each with one (not stacked) box-shadow. No canvas is used for indent guides specifically.
Source: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/src/vs/editor/browser/viewParts/indentGuides/indentGuides.ts

**2. CM6 `@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers` — CONFIRMED single-element/stacked-gradient technique.** One line-decoration (`class: 'cm-indent-markers'`) per line sets a CSS custom property to a comma-joined list of `repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers (one per marker segment: before-active, active, after-active), consumed by a single `::before`:
```js
const gradient = `repeating-linear-gradient(to right, var(${markerCssProperty}) 0 ${thickness}px, transparent ${thickness}px ${indentWidth}ch)`
```
```css
'.cm-indent-markers::before': { position: 'absolute', background: 'var(--indent-markers)', ... }
```
So it's O(1) DOM elements per line regardless of depth. Source: https://github.com/replit/codemirror-indentation-markers/blob/master/src/index.ts

**3. Other concrete examples — not found (flag).** Search turned up only generic multi-box-shadow docs (https://github.com/jbranchaud/til/blob/master/css/apply-multiple-box-shadows-to-single-element.md, https://css-tricks.com/snippets/css/css-box-shadow/), not one applied specifically to indent guides.

**4. Assessment (my reasoning, inferred):** stacked-layer wins on DOM-node count (O(1) vs O(depth)) and ease of depth changes (regenerate one string vs create/destroy nodes); it loses on CSS-declarative flexibility — colors/widths are baked into a JS-computed string rather than overridable per-level via CSS classes (VS Code's per-div approach supports 6 rotating bracket colors purely via `lvl-N` selectors). Both scale fine with variable line height since extents use relative/`ch` units anchored to the line box.</result>

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>## Research findings: CM6-based outliner unification prior art

**1. Foam** — Confirmed VS Code extension, and VS Code's editor is Monaco, not CodeMirror. ([github.com/foambubble/foam](https://github.com/foambubble/foam)) No CM6 relevance; its only outline-adjacent feature is a separate graph visualization panel, not in-editor heading/list unification. ([foam docs](https://github.com/foambubble/foam/blob/main/docs/user/features/graph-visualization.md))

**2. Dendron** — Also a VS Code/Monaco extension. ([marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=dendron.dendron](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=dendron.dendron)) No CM6 involvement found.

**3. Athens Research** — Repo is explicitly archived: "Athens is no longer being actively maintained." ([github.com/athensresearch/athens](https://github.com/athensresearch/athens)) It's a ClojureScript/re-frame/DataScript app; no CM6 usage found — its block editor was a custom contenteditable implementation, not a general markdown/CM6 editor, so it's architecturally irrelevant as CM6 prior art (though not conclusively documented in the README itself).

**4. Logseq** — Not CM6-based for its primary block editor. CodeMirror only appears as an optional embedded editor for fenced code blocks (`:editor/extra-codemirror-options`, CM5 `keyMap` options). ([discuss.logseq.com](https://discuss.logseq.com/t/getting-the-keymap-option-to-work-for-editor-extra-codemirror-options/21873)) Main blocks use a custom per-block textarea/contenteditable model, not CM6.

**5. Niche CM6 projects**: `codemirror-orgmode` (mickael-kerjean) uses CodeMirror 5 (`^5.40.0` in package.json), not CM6. `obsidian-orgmode-cm6` (BBazard) IS CM6-based but only reimplements org-mode syntax highlighting/folding as an isolated instance — no cross-kind heading/paragraph/list unification. ([github.com/BBazard/obsidian-orgmode-cm6](https://github.com/BBazard/obsidian-orgmode-cm6))

**Closest real analog**: `obsidian-outliner` (vslinko), CM6-based since Obsidian uses CM6. Discussion #53 shows a direct request to unify headings into the bullet-outline hierarchy — the maintainer explicitly declined ("One Thing Well" philosophy), leaving headings/paragraphs unintegrated. ([github.com/vslinko/obsidian-outliner/discussions/53](https://github.com/vslinko/obsidian-outliner/discussions/53))

**Bottom line**: No project was found that achieves full cross-kind (heading+paragraph+list) visual indentation unification atop a general CM6 markdown editor — the closest CM6 candidate (obsidian-outliner) explicitly scoped headings out.</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>57646</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>22</tool_uses><duration_ms>146269</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

Discussion #53 shows a direct request to unify headings into the bullet-outline hierarchy — the maintainer explicitly declined ("One Thing Well" philosophy), leaving headings/paragraphs unintegrated. ([github.com/vslinko/obsidian-outliner/discussions/53](https://github.com/vslinko/obsidian-outliner/discussions/53))

**Bottom line**: No project was found that achieves full cross-kind (heading+paragraph+list) visual indentation unification atop a general CM6 markdown editor — the closest CM6 candidate (obsidian-outliner) explicitly scoped headings out.</result>

 | [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md](03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md) | Can the experience be built on public APIs only? Architecture options, guidelines/scorecard constraints, verdict |
 | [04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md) | Decisions that need alignment before any planning/spec work |
 | [05-org-mode-comparison.md](05-org-mode-comparison.md) | Where our mapping algebra aligns with / diverges from org-mode, the closest living reference system |
+| [06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md) | Postmortem on the failed `outline-decorations` visual-chrome attempt: what was tried, why the CSS-override strategy kept breaking, and why the testing approach gave false confidence |
 
 ## TL;DR
 
diff --git a/e2e/helpers.ts b/e2e/helpers.ts
index dfe3e06..886162c 100644
--- a/e2e/helpers.ts
+++ b/e2e/helpers.ts
@@ -214,6 +214,62 @@ export async function dismissNotices(): Promise<void> {
   });
 }
 
+// ---- Decorations ------------------------------------------------------------
+
+export interface LineDecorationState {
+  /** `to-node-line` present: a text-flow kind (heading/paragraph/list-item). */
+  hasIndent: boolean;
+  /** `to-node-atom` present: an atom (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr). */
+  hasAtomIndent: boolean;
+  /** Synthetic `::before` marker (paragraphs/headings/atoms without a native one). */
+  hasMarker: boolean;
+  /** Native marker glyph (list bullet/number) restyled in place, no synthetic marker added. */
+  hasNativeMarker: boolean;
+  depth: number | null;
+}
+
+/** Read the rendered CM6 line decorations (class + --to-depth) in document order. */
+export function getLineDecorations(): Promise<LineDecorationState[]> {
+  return browser.execute(() => {
+    // Scope to the active leaf: multiple panes/hidden leaves can each carry
+    // their own `.cm-content`, and an unscoped query would concatenate them.
+    const lines = Array.from(
+      document.querySelectorAll('.workspace-leaf.mod-active .cm-content > .cm-line'),
+    );
+    return lines.map((el) => {
+      const style = (el as HTMLElement).style;
+      const depthRaw = style.getPropertyValue('--to-depth').trim();
+      return {
+        hasIndent: el.classList.contains('to-node-line'),
+        hasAtomIndent: el.classList.contains('to-node-atom'),
+        hasMarker: el.classList.contains('to-node-marker'),
+        hasNativeMarker: el.classList.contains('to-node-native-marker'),
+        depth: depthRaw === '' ? null : Number(depthRaw),
+      };
+    });
+  });
+}
+
+/**
+ * Read each active-leaf line's computed `padding-left`/`margin-left`, in
+ * document order — used to compare actual rendered indentation across node
+ * kinds (e.g. asserting a heading and a list item at the same tree depth
+ * render the same padding-left).
+ */
+export function getComputedLinePaddings(): Promise<
+  { paddingLeft: string; marginLeft: string }[]
+> {
+  return browser.execute(() => {
+    const lines = Array.from(
+      document.querySelectorAll('.workspace-leaf.mod-active .cm-content > .cm-line'),
+    );
+    return lines.map((el) => {
+      const cs = getComputedStyle(el);
+      return { paddingLeft: cs.paddingLeft, marginLeft: cs.marginLeft };
+    });
+  });
+}
+
 // ---- Keys -----------------------------------------------------------------
 
 const PRIMARY_MOD = process.platform === 'darwin' ? Key.Command : Key.Ctrl;
diff --git a/openspec/changes/archive/2026-07-13-editor-core/verification.md b/openspec/changes/archive/2026-07-13-editor-core/verification.md
index 2286811..5d24021 100644
--- a/openspec/changes/archive/2026-07-13-editor-core/verification.md
+++ b/openspec/changes/archive/2026-07-13-editor-core/verification.md
@@ -41,6 +41,19 @@ shares is automated.)
 - Coexistence warning (via stub `obsidian-outliner` plugin): once, not after
   restart
 
+### Node chrome decorations → `e2e/specs/50-decorations.e2e.ts`
+
+- No decorations off outline mode; appear immediately on toggle, no reload
+  or edit needed
+- Indentation-guide depth agrees across heading level, list indent, and
+  paragraph-adjacency encodings (tree position, not raw markdown)
+- Paragraph leader marker on paragraph nodes only; list items, headings,
+  and atoms keep their native rendering, no synthetic marker
+- Flat paragraph-only documents read as nodes (the dev-vault finding this
+  change was built to fix)
+- Non-mutation: document text, cursor, and undo stack unaffected by
+  decoration rendering or recompute
+
 ## Manual residue
 
 - [ ] Mobile smoke (optional this change): plugin loads on iOS/Android
@@ -51,3 +64,6 @@ shares is automated.)
       console clean of `[true-outliner] parse disagreement` (automatable
       later by reading the console through the harness; any hit → task 4.2)
 - [ ] Notice appearance/timing feels right (pure UX judgment)
+- [ ] Decorations theme sweep: indentation guides and the paragraph marker
+      read cleanly at 3+ nesting levels, in both the bundled light and dark
+      themes (outline-decorations change)

<output-file>/private/tmp/claude-502/-Users-laughedelic-Code-obsidian-true-outliner/5d2fe435-402d-4ce3-b21f-babbd7282d42/tasks/a874fc135f08d14ba.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Research CM6 widget-as-spacer technique" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>## Prior art for CM6 widget-as-indentation-spacer

**1. Official docs/examples — no exact match.** CM6's official decoration example (https://codemirror.net/examples/decoration/) shows only a `CheckboxWidget` and `PlaceholderWidget` (`side: 1`, `EditorView.atomicRanges`) — no width-spacer example. `Decoration.widget`'s `side` option is documented generically ("Which side of the given position the widget is on... Defaults to 0", per `@codemirror/view` types) with no indentation use case shown. The gutter/line-number system is a separate `gutter()`/`GutterMarker` API, unrelated to inline widgets-at-position-0 — no official conflation found.

**2. Community prior art.** Closest hits are about indentation *guides*, not content-pushing: in https://discuss.codemirror.net/t/indentation-guides/3797/6 maintainer Marijn suggests widget decorations holding styled spaces to extend guide lines across blank lines, and describes "view plugins that add... -1px margin widgets after whitespace." A concrete implementation (https://gist.github.com/s-cork/898cd7c21e55a4963132cb9d9e2c6615) places a widget (`side: 1`) whose `textContent = " ".repeat(indent)` on empty lines — structurally close but uses space characters, not `style.width`.

**Negative evidence:** the most popular real project, replit's `codemirror-indentation-markers` (https://github.com/replit/codemirror-indentation-markers), uses `Decoration.line` + CSS gradients, zero widgets. The community-standard hanging-indent technique (https://discuss.codemirror.net/t/making-codemirror-6-respect-indent-for-wrapped-lines/2881) uses `text-indent`/`padding-left` via `Decoration.line`. **COULD NOT CONFIRM** any Obsidian plugin (checked obsidian-outliner, codemirror-rich-obsidian, official Obsidian decoration docs) doing exactly a px-width spacer widget — the ecosystem converged on CSS-on-the-line instead.

**3. Copy/paste exclusion — CONFIRMED.** `copiedRange()` in `view/src/input.ts` (https://github.com/codemirror/view/blob/main/src/input.ts) builds clipboard text via `state.sliceDoc()`/`doc.lineAt`, never DOM traversal. Widgets never enter `EditorState.doc`, so they're excluded from copy by architecture. Layout-neutrality of a real inline DOM node with `style.width` is **INFERRED, not explicitly documented**.

**4. Gotchas — confirmed:** cursor/backspace glitches adjacent to widgets (archived issue https://github.com/codemirror/dev/issues/111, requires two backspaces); CM6 wraps every widget in an invisible `<img>`-based `.cm-widgetBuffer` for contenteditable-bug workarounds, which itself causes unwanted wrap points — maintainer confirmed no clean fix (https://discuss.codemirror.net/t/hiding-widget-buffers-for-better-text-wrapping/7512). Per-line-widget performance and z-index/overlap conflicts: **COULD NOT CONFIRM**, no benchmarks/reports found.</result>

</task-notification>

<result>## Org-indent-mode / line-prefix / wrap-prefix research report

**1. line-prefix vs wrap-prefix (confirmed — GNU Emacs Lisp Reference Manual, "Special Properties")**
https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Special-Properties.html

> "If a region of text has a `line-prefix` property, the prefix it defines will be added at display time to the beginning of every non-continuation line."
> "If a region of text has a `wrap-prefix` property, the prefix it defines will be added at display time to the beginning of every continuation line due to text wrapping... the `wrap-prefix` property must be set on the entire region of text, starting from the first character of the first line... otherwise... the display engine checks for this property only immediately after continuing a line."
> "The property value may be a string or an image..., or a stretch of whitespace such as specified by the `:width` or `:align-to` display properties."

So: `line-prefix` = first visual row of a logical line; `wrap-prefix` = every soft-wrapped continuation row of that same logical line. Both are pure display-time overlays — no buffer characters inserted.

**2. How org-indent-mode applies them (confirmed — org-indent.el, emacs-straight mirror)**
https://github.com/emacs-straight/org-mode/blob/main/lisp/org-indent.el

It uses **plain space strings**, not `:width` stretch specs. `org-indent-set-line-properties` builds `line`/`wrap` via `make-string` of `?\s` (scaled by `org-indent-indentation-per-level`, default 2/level), faces them, then:
```elisp
(add-text-properties (line-beginning-position) (line-beginning-position 2)
  `(line-prefix ,line wrap-prefix ,wrap))
```
This is driven per-line by `org-indent-add-properties`, called synchronously on every buffer edit; only the *initial* full-buffer scan is deferred/interruptible via idle timers (`org-indent-agent-resume-timer`, `throw 'interrupt`). Per the file's own Commentary: "This is an implementation of dynamic virtual indentation... The process is synchronous, toggled at every buffer modification. Though, the initialization... which can take a few seconds in large buffers, happens on idle time." The org manual (https://orgmode.org/manual/Org-Indent-Mode.html) confirms it also sets `org-hide-leading-stars` and explicitly "sets `wrap-prefix` correctly for indenting and wrapping long lines of headlines or text."

**3. Performance and bugs (confirmed via mailing list / bug tracker)**
- 2019 org-mode ML report: toggling checkboxes across ~150 list items got noticeably slower with the mode on (https://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/emacs-orgmode/2019-09/msg00037.html) — no root-cause diagnosis found in the thread itself; consistent with the synchronous-on-every-edit design noted above (my inference, not stated in that thread).
- Bug#41584 (confirmed, https://orgmode.org/list/83d06mm09n.fsf@gnu.org/T/): "The line-prefix text property set by org-indent-mode sometimes vanishes when typing near a line where an overlay is applied" (e.g. with electric-pair-mode). Eli Zaretskii traced it to a redisplay window-reuse optimization bug in xdisp.c, fixed on master, **not backported** to release branches.
- Spacemacs issue #1418 (confirmed, https://github.com/syl20bnr/spacemacs/issues/1418): enabling `adaptive-wrap-prefix-mode` alongside `org-indent-mode` "stops visual line from indenting properly" — two packages fighting over the same `wrap-prefix` slot. This illustrates a structural limitation worth flagging for CM6 design: these properties are single-valued per buffer position, not composable across independent features (this generalization is my inference from the bug report, not stated verbatim in it).

**4. CM6 equivalent (no exact analog found — flagged as absence, not proven non-existence)**
CM6's `Decoration.line` attaches classes/attributes to a whole rendered line but has no built-in split between "first visual row" vs "wrapped continuation row" content the way Emacs's redisplay engine does natively. Community threads on discuss.codemirror.net ("Making Codemirror 6 respect indent for wrapped lines," https://discuss.codemirror.net/t/making-codemirror-6-respect-indent-for-wrapped-lines/2881; "Hanging Indent," https://discuss.codemirror.net/t/hanging-indent/243) converge on a CSS approximation instead: a line `Decoration` sets `text-indent: calc(-1 * N)` plus `padding-left`/`margin-left: N`, pushing wrapped rows in by N while the true first-row start stays put — later revised to `::before` pseudo-elements + `border-left` because negative `text-indent` broke rectangular-selection hit-testing (documented bug in that same thread). I searched explicitly for any CM6 GitHub/discuss thread citing Emacs's line-prefix/wrap-prefix as inspiration and **found none** — noting this as an absence given my search coverage, not a confirmed fact that no such discussion exists anywhere. The nearest true structural analog for "virtual content before line start" in CM6 is a start-of-line `Decoration.widget` (side: -1), but that only covers the first row, not continuation rows — CM6 has no first-row/continuation-row distinction, which is the real gap versus Emacs (my assessment).</result>
